verses.text: And it came to pass, when Saul was returned from following the Philistines, that it was told him, saying, Behold, David is in the wilderness of En-gedi. Then Saul took three thousand chosen men out of all Israel, and went to seek David and his men upon the rocks of the wild goats. Guidance: Saul finishes off the Philistine emergency that interrupted the chase in chapter 23, and immediately picks up where he left off. The intelligence arrives — David is at En-gedi — and Saul mobilizes three thousand 'chosen men' (the elite, hand-picked core of Israel's army) to hunt one fugitive band of six hundred. The disproportion is grotesque. Three thousand picked soldiers against six hundred half-armed wilderness men, for the personal grievance of the king. The chapter is showing us a kingdom whose security apparatus has been turned against itself. 'The rocks of the wild goats' is a stunning location — sheer limestone cliffs above En-gedi where ibex still roam today; only goats can navigate them naturally. Saul takes three thousand soldiers into terrain made for goats. The very landscape of En-gedi is going to make the king's prey accessible only at the cost of total operational disadvantage. God's geography has its own opinion about who is hunting whom.

And it came to pass, when Saul was returned from following the Philistines, that it was told him, saying, Behold, David is in the wilderness of En-gedi. Then Saul took three thousand chosen men out of all Israel, and went to seek David and his men upon the rocks of the wild goats. Guidance: Saul finishes off the Philistine emergency that interrupted the chase in chapter 23, and immediately picks up where he left off. The intelligence arrives — David is at En-gedi — and Saul mobilizes three thousand 'chosen men' (the elite, hand-picked core of Israel's army) to hunt one fugitive band of six hundred. The disproportion is grotesque. Three thousand picked soldiers against six hundred half-armed wilderness men, for the personal grievance of the king. The chapter is showing us a kingdom whose security apparatus has been turned against itself. 'The rocks of the wild goats' is a stunning location — sheer limestone cliffs above En-gedi where ibex still roam today; only goats can navigate them naturally. Saul takes three thousand soldiers into terrain made for goats. The very landscape of En-gedi is going to make the king's prey accessible only at the cost of total operational disadvantage. God's geography has its own opinion about who is hunting whom.

And he came to the sheepcotes by the way, where was a cave; and Saul went in to cover his feet: and David and his men remained in the sides of the cave. Guidance: Saul stops at some sheep-pens along the trail. Beside them is a cave. Saul enters alone 'to cover his feet' — the Hebrew euphemism for relieving himself (the long robe of an Israelite was draped around the legs in the squat). The detail is meant to make us feel the vulnerability of the moment. The king is at his most physically exposed — undressed, defenseless, his guard outside. And David and his six hundred men are at the back of the same cave, hidden in the deeper recesses. En-gedi caves run hundreds of feet into the rock; a man at the mouth would see daylight and not the figures in the shadows behind him. The most powerful man in the kingdom has just walked unarmed into the resting place of the only band of men who could kill him. Providence is sometimes a stagecraft of small humiliations: the king who has been hunting God's anointed walks into the hunted man's bathroom.

And the men of David said unto him, Behold the day of which the LORD said unto thee, Behold, I will deliver thine enemy into thine hand, that thou mayest do to him as it shall seem good unto thee. Then David arose, and cut off the skirt of Saul's robe privily. Guidance: David's men whisper urgently. They interpret the moment theologically: 'behold the day of which the LORD said.' Notice — there is no record of God ever saying this. The men have invented (or remembered selectively) a promise; the LORD never explicitly told David 'I will deliver Saul to thee that thou mayest kill him.' They are reading their own opportunity as divine will. This is a constant temptation among well-meaning followers of God's anointed: to attribute to God the convenient interpretation of an opportunity. David moves — but not as they suggested. He sneaks up behind the squatting king and cuts off a piece of the hem of the royal robe. The 'skirt' (kanaph) is the prominent corner of an outer garment, often elaborately decorated, signifying authority and status. (The same word will recur in Ruth 3:9 — 'spread thy skirt over me' — as the symbol of marital covering.) David takes the symbol of royalty, not the life of the king. The act is precise: he is acquiring proof that he was close enough to kill, without taking the kill. Faith does not always do the obvious thing the moment requires; sometimes it does the more careful, more restrained thing that proves a point without committing the sin.

And it came to pass afterward, that David's heart smote him, because he had cut off Saul's skirt. And he said unto his men, The LORD forbid that I should do this thing unto my master, the LORD's anointed, to stretch forth mine hand against him, seeing he is the anointed of the LORD. Guidance: Even the restrained act stings David's conscience. 'David's heart smote him' — the Hebrew is yak (struck) — the same verb used of Doeg striking down the priests. David's conscience itself is the smiter. To cut the royal robe was, in the political symbolism of the period, to figuratively un-king the wearer (this is how Samuel read the tearing of his own robe back in 15:27-28). David has just enacted a small symbolic act of kingdom-tearing against the LORD's anointed king, and even that small symbolism is too much for him. His explanation to his men is one of the great political-theological statements of the Old Testament: 'The LORD forbid that I should do this thing unto my master, the LORD's anointed, to stretch forth mine hand against him, seeing he is the anointed of the LORD.' Three times in two sentences he calls Saul 'the LORD's anointed.' Saul has been morally disqualified; God has rejected him; the kingdom is going to David — and David still calls him 'the LORD's anointed' and refuses to lift a hand against him. The principle: God's own decision to set someone aside is not a license for me to set them aside violently. The transition belongs to God, not to me. David's restraint here will become the model for all future biblical thinking about how God's chosen ones should relate to authorities God has rejected but not yet replaced — quietly, with respect for the office, refusing the temptation to accelerate God's timing through one's own hand.

His explanation to his men is one of the great political-theological statements of the Old Testament: 'The LORD forbid that I should do this thing unto my master, the LORD's anointed, to stretch forth mine hand against him, seeing he is the anointed of the LORD.' David's restraint here will become the model for all future biblical thinking about how God's chosen ones should relate to authorities God has rejected but not yet replaced — quietly, with respect for the office, refusing the temptation to accelerate God's timing through one's own hand.

So David stayed his servants with these words, and suffered them not to rise against Saul. But Saul rose up out of the cave, and went on his way. Guidance: 'David stayed his servants with these words.' The verb is strong — he restrained them; they did not go forward. Note the implication: without David's restraint, his men would have killed Saul. David's leadership in this moment is not toward a victory; it is *against* a victory his men were eagerly ready to pursue. Sometimes the most decisive thing a leader does is stop his own people from doing the easy expedient thing. The juxtaposition of the verse is unforgettable: 'Saul rose up out of the cave, and went on his way.' He leaves alive without ever knowing how close he was. He resumes his hunt, oblivious to the mercy he just received. The most powerful mercies in our lives are sometimes the ones we never realize we received. David, watching Saul walk away with his robe a little shorter than it was, has just done one of the holiest acts of his life — and Saul, never the wiser, is at this moment still planning to kill him. Mercy is hidden until the spared person sees it; until then, it is just one more invisible act of restraint on someone else's part.

David also arose afterward, and went out of the cave, and cried after Saul, saying, My lord the king. And when Saul looked behind him, David stooped with his face to the earth, and bowed himself. Guidance: David does not let the moment pass anonymously. He emerges from the cave, calls after Saul, and bows to the ground. 'My lord the king' — the most deferential possible address. He could have stayed quiet, let the king walk away, kept the secret. He chooses to surface the secret and use the proof as the basis for an honest conversation. The bow is also choreographed: it is the prostration of a vassal before a lord. David is publicly enacting that the kingdom's hierarchy is still in force, even though the kingdom's king is wrong. He is giving Saul the chance to see what just happened. Sometimes the noblest thing one can do with restraint is not hide it but offer it to the other party as evidence of the relationship still possible.

And David said to Saul, Wherefore hearest thou men's words, saying, Behold, David seeketh thy hurt? Behold, this day thine eyes have seen how that the LORD had delivered thee today into mine hand in the cave: and some bade me kill thee: but mine eye spared thee; and I said, I will not put forth mine hand against my lord; for he is the LORD's anointed. Guidance: The speech begins with a question David has been waiting years to ask: 'wherefore hearest thou men's words?' Saul's hatred has been built on whispers (the Ziphites' informants, Doeg's report, the women's song misread). David names the source of the rupture and asks Saul to consider whether the source is reliable. Then comes the evidence: 'this day thine eyes have seen.' What Saul has just lived through is the proof. 'The LORD had delivered thee today into mine hand' — David inverts Saul's earlier phrase from 23:7 ('God hath delivered him into mine hand'). The verb is the same; Saul was wrong about who was being delivered. David's testimony names two things: (1) the opportunity was real and recognized by his men; (2) the restraint was his deliberate choice, motivated by Saul's office. He had every reason to kill and every excuse for doing so, and he did not. The argument is the strongest possible refutation of Saul's premise. David is not just a non-threat; David is the proof Saul could examine that the premise of his pursuit is a lie.

And David said to Saul, Wherefore hearest thou men's words, saying, Behold, David seeketh thy hurt? Behold, this day thine eyes have seen how that the LORD had delivered thee today into mine hand in the cave: and some bade me kill thee: but mine eye spared thee; and I said, I will not put forth mine hand against my lord; for he is the LORD's anointed. Guidance: The speech begins with a question David has been waiting years to ask: 'wherefore hearest thou men's words?' Saul's hatred has been built on whispers (the Ziphites' informants, Doeg's report, the women's song misread). David names the source of the rupture and asks Saul to consider whether the source is reliable. Then comes the evidence: 'this day thine eyes have seen.' What Saul has just lived through is the proof. 'The LORD had delivered thee today into mine hand' — David inverts Saul's earlier phrase from 23:7 ('God hath delivered him into mine hand'). The verb is the same; Saul was wrong about who was being delivered. David's testimony names two things: (1) the opportunity was real and recognized by his men; (2) the restraint was his deliberate choice, motivated by Saul's office. He had every reason to kill and every excuse for doing so, and he did not. The argument is the strongest possible refutation of Saul's premise. David is not just a non-threat; David is the proof Saul could examine that the premise of his pursuit is a lie.

Moreover, my father, see, yea, see the skirt of thy robe in my hand: for in that I cut off the skirt of thy robe, and killed thee not, know thou and see that there is neither evil nor transgression in mine hand, and I have not sinned against thee; yet thou huntest my soul to take it. Guidance: David lifts the corner of the robe and holds it up. 'My father, see, yea, see.' The reduplication is emotional; David is begging Saul to look. The address 'my father' is striking. Saul is his father-in-law (Michal's father), yes, but it is more than that. David is invoking the relational tenderness that, for all the spear-throwing, used to exist between them. He is calling Saul to the relationship the office is supposed to provide. The piece of cloth proves three things at once: (1) David had the opportunity (he was that close); (2) David had self-control (he limited his action to the hem); (3) David is not in moral debt (he can show the evidence in his hand). The closing line is the chapter's most poignant: 'yet thou huntest my soul to take it.' The contrast cannot be sharper. David spared the life that was at his mercy; Saul is hunting the life that has done him only good. The asymmetry is on display for everyone to see.

The LORD judge between me and thee, and the LORD avenge me of thee: but mine hand shall not be upon thee. As saith the proverb of the ancients, Wickedness proceedeth from the wicked: but mine hand shall not be upon thee. Guidance: David hands the dispute to God. 'The LORD judge between me and thee, and the LORD avenge me of thee.' He is invoking divine arbitration as both judge and executioner. But the second clause is the more striking: 'but mine hand shall not be upon thee.' Twice in two sentences he repeats it — 'mine hand shall not be upon thee.' Vengeance is the LORD's job, not David's. The hand that could have struck will not strike. This is one of the foundational sentences in Old Testament political ethics: the legitimacy of God's justice does not bend over to be exercised through my hand against a person God Himself has not authorized me to strike. Even when I am the wronged party. Especially then. Then David quotes 'the proverb of the ancients': 'wickedness proceedeth from the wicked.' Translation: a wicked man will not refrain from wickedness; therefore, the very evidence that I did not kill you is the evidence that I am not wicked. If I were the man you say I am, you would now be dead. The argument is airtight. David has constructed an ironclad legal-theological case in the open air, with the king staring at him.

'The LORD judge between me and thee, and the LORD avenge me of thee.' He is invoking divine arbitration as both judge and executioner. Vengeance is the LORD's job, not David's. The hand that could have struck will not strike. Even when I am the wronged party. Especially then. Then David quotes 'the proverb of the ancients': 'wickedness proceedeth from the wicked.' If I were the man you say I am, you would now be dead. The argument is airtight. David has constructed an ironclad legal-theological case in the open air, with the king staring at him.

After whom is the king of Israel come out? after whom dost thou pursue? after a dead dog, after a flea. The LORD therefore be judge, and judge between me and thee, and see, and plead my cause, and deliver me out of thine hand. Guidance: David shifts to self-deprecation, but it is also dignified protest. 'After whom is the king of Israel come out?' The Hebrew implies — who am I that the king himself, with three thousand picked men, hunts me? 'After a dead dog, after a flea.' Two self-images. The dead dog is the most worthless object imaginable in ancient Near-Eastern culture — a despised, polluting carcass. The flea is the smallest annoyance; a thing too tiny to be worth chasing. David is asking Saul to see how absurd his pursuit looks. The king of a nation expending his elite troops to chase a flea. The size of the king's grievance is not proportionate to the size of the supposed threat. Sometimes the most cutting critique of someone's hostility is simply to ask them, gently, what exactly they think they are pursuing. Then David closes the speech with the same legal request: 'the LORD therefore be judge… and plead my cause, and deliver me out of thine hand.' He has spoken; he is now done; he leaves the court of heaven to issue the verdict.

And it came to pass, when David had made an end of speaking these words unto Saul, that Saul said, Is this thy voice, my son David? And Saul lifted up his voice, and wept. And he said to David, Thou art more righteous than I: for thou hast rewarded me good, whereas I have rewarded thee evil. Guidance: The transformation in Saul lasts only this scene, but the scene itself is one of the most extraordinary moments in 1 Samuel. 'Is this thy voice, my son David?' The 'my son' is the language Saul used in chapter 18 when he loved David. The hostility breaks for a moment, and the king sees the man. 'Saul lifted up his voice, and wept.' Open, audible, public weeping — the kind that is impossible to suppress. Then the great confession: 'Thou art more righteous than I: for thou hast rewarded me good, whereas I have rewarded thee evil.' Saul says aloud what he has known for chapters. David is more righteous. Saul's pursuit has been evil; David's restraint has been good. This is what genuine repentance sounds like — naming who has done what, in what direction, without softening. For a brief moment the chapter holds out the hope that Saul might actually turn. The next chapter will show whether the moment was lasting. (It was not. In chapter 26, Saul will be hunting David again.) But for this single afternoon at the cave mouth, the truth is on the king's lips and the tears are real.

And it came to pass, when David had made an end of speaking these words unto Saul, that Saul said, Is this thy voice, my son David? And Saul lifted up his voice, and wept. And thou hast shewed this day how that thou hast dealt well with me: forasmuch as when the LORD had delivered me into thine hand, thou killedst me not. For if a man find his enemy, will he let him go well away? wherefore the LORD reward thee good for that thou hast done unto me this day. Guidance: Saul names the act precisely: 'when the LORD had delivered me into thine hand, thou killedst me not.' Saul finally agrees with David's theology of who delivered whom into whose hand. Then the rhetorical question: 'if a man find his enemy, will he let him go well away?' Saul names the unnaturalness of what David did. Ordinary men do not spare ordinary enemies. David's act has no political precedent in this region in this era. Then the blessing: 'wherefore the LORD reward thee good for that thou hast done unto me this day.' Saul prays God's blessing on the man he came to kill. It is one of the few unselfish prayers Saul has spoken in the book. The chapter is showing that even the most hardened heart can experience a moment of clarity when confronted with concrete mercy. The hostility ferments long; one act of grace can crack it open. The cracking does not always last. But for this moment, it is real, and the man Saul has been hunting hears the man Saul might have been speaking again.

For if a man find his enemy, will he let him go well away? wherefore the LORD reward thee good for that thou hast done unto me this day. And now, behold, I know well that thou shalt surely be king, and that the kingdom of Israel shall be established in thine hand. Guidance: A stunning admission. The king of Israel publicly tells his successor: 'I know well that thou shalt surely be king, and that the kingdom of Israel shall be established in thine hand.' Saul finally names what he has been refusing to name for many chapters. Samuel told him in chapter 15. Jonathan told David in chapter 23. Now Saul himself, with three thousand soldiers within earshot, acknowledges it. The political consequences of this admission are enormous; if Saul's officers heard it (and they probably did, given the open-air setting), then Saul has effectively endorsed his rival's claim. Yet Saul, in this moment, names truth. He has, even in his fallen state, retained enough capacity for honesty under pressure to say what he knows. He just cannot — will not — act on that knowledge once the emotional moment passes. The chapter shows us a category of person whose problem is not perception but the gulf between perception and conduct. Many of us have been him. The truth was on our lips and the next day we lived as if we had never said it.

Swear now therefore unto me by the LORD, that thou wilt not cut off my seed after me, and that thou wilt not destroy my name out of my father's house. And David sware unto Saul. And Saul went home; but David and his men gat them up unto the hold. Guidance: Saul asks the same favor Jonathan asked in 20:14-15 — preservation of his descendants and his name. 'Swear now therefore unto me by the LORD, that thou wilt not cut off my seed after me.' The standard practice of new dynasties was to exterminate the old royal line; Saul knows this and is asking David to make an exception. David swears. The vow will be kept — David will not kill Saul's descendants when the kingdom passes; he will care for Mephibosheth (2 Sam 9), and the only Saulide deaths under his reign will be ones he opposed (e.g., Ish-bosheth's murder in 2 Sam 4, which David explicitly condemns; the Gibeonite execution in 2 Sam 21, in which David spares Mephibosheth specifically). 'Saul went home; but David and his men gat them up unto the hold.' The chapter closes with separation. Saul goes back to Gibeah; David and his men return to the wilderness strongholds. The moment of reconciliation does not bridge the political reality. They both know what comes next — Saul will not give up the throne, David will not return to court, and the LORD will work out the transition in His own way and time. Even after the most extraordinary scene of recognition and tears, the two men remain on opposite sides of the same kingdom. Sometimes the deepest reconciliations one can achieve in this life are still bounded by the structural problems that no apology can dissolve. The hope of those reconciliations lies in a final day God Himself will arrange, where every Saul who confessed and every David who waited will sit together at one table without the office between them.
